description:
A developer is tidying up some helper scripts inside an existing project
    directory and wants to make sure that a basic DNS-lookup helper works
    correctly before expanding it.  
    Your goal: produce a one–line terminal command that     1) resolves the IPv4
    address for the hostname “localhost” **only**,     2) writes the result to the
    file  

        /home/user/projects/project-lambda/resolution.log  

    in the exact format shown below, overwriting the file if it already exists.

    Required output format inside /home/user/projects/project-lambda/resolution.log
    (including the final newline character):

        localhost:127.0.0.1

    • No extra spaces are allowed anywhere in the line.  
    • There must be exactly one newline after the line (i.e. the file ends with a
      trailing newline).  
    • The file must contain this single line and nothing else.  

    Restrictions & hints:  
    • Use any standard command-line DNS/hostname-resolution utility available in
      a typical Linux distribution; keep it to one compound shell line.  
    • Do not edit /etc/hosts or any system files; just query and capture the
      information.  
    • The directory /home/user/projects/project-lambda/ already exists and is
      writable, so no extra directories need to be created.  
    • The expected IPv4 address for “localhost” is 127.0.0.1.  
    • Remember to overwrite rather than append, and make sure the output matches
      the required format exactly so that automated tests can verify your work.
truth:
<!--
        The automated checker will verify that, after the agent finishes,
        the file /home/user/projects/project-lambda/resolution.log exists and
        contains exactly the following three characters plus a newline:

            localhost:127.0.0.1\n

        Directory that already exists when the exercise starts:
            /home/user/projects/project-lambda/

        No other files or directories are required for verification.
    -->
